content_markdown:
The kinetic energy $E_{\text{K}}$ of a satellite in orbit varies with its distance $r$ from the centre of a planet of radius $R$.

Which **one** of the following graphs best shows the variation of $E_{\text{K}}$ with $r$?

diagram_metadata.description: Four graphs showing Kinetic Energy (Ek) on the y-axis and distance (r) on the x-axis. Each graph starts at r=R (indicated by a vertical dashed line). Graph A shows a linear increase. Graph B shows a reciprocal/inverse curve decreasing towards zero. Graph C shows a logarithmic-style curve increasing. Graph D shows a linear decrease.

vibe_explanation: For a satellite in a circular orbit, the centripetal force is provided by gravity: $G M m / r^2 = m v^2 / r$. This simplifies to $m v^2 = G M m / r$. Since kinetic energy $E_{\text{K}} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$, we find that $E_{\text{K}} = G M m / (2r)$. This means $E_{\text{K}}$ is proportional to $1/r$, which is represented by a decreasing curve that approaches the x-axis asymptotically, as shown in graph B.
